content.problemStatement:
CodeChef recently revamped its practice page to make it easier for users to identify the next problems they should solve by introducing some new features:


Recent Contest Problems - contains only problems from the last 2 contests
Separate Un-Attempted,  Attempted, and All tabs
Problem Difficulty Rating - the Recommended dropdown menu has various difficulty ranges so that you can attempt the problems most suited to your experience
Popular Topics and Tags
Like most users, Chef didn’t know that he could add problems to a personal to-do list by clicking on the magic **'+'** symbol on the top-right of each problem page. But once he found out about it, he went crazy and added loads of problems to his to-do list without looking at their difficulty rating.

Chef is a beginner and should ideally try and solve only problems with difficulty rating strictly less than 100010001000. Given a list of difficulty ratings for problems in the Chef’s to-do list, please help him identify how many of those problems Chef should **remove** from his to-do list, so that he is only left with problems of difficulty rating less than 100010001000.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase consists of 2 lines of input.
The first line of input of each test case contains a single integer, NNN, which is the total number of problems that the Chef has added to his to-do list.
The second line of input of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers D1,D2,…,DND_1, D_2, \ldots, D_ND1​,D2​,…,DN​, which are the difficulty ratings for each problem in the to-do list.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the number of problems that Chef will have to remove so that all remaining problems have a difficulty rating strictly less than 100010001000.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of problems in Chef's to‑do list. The second line contains N space‑separated integers D1,D2,…,DN – the difficulty ratings of the problems.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of problems Chef has to remove so that every remaining problem has difficulty strictly less than 1000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: only 1200 ≥ 1000, so remove 1 problem.
Test case 2: 1000,1001,1002 are ≥1000, remove 3.
Test case 3: only 5000 ≥1000, remove 1.
Test case 4: all five values are ≥1000, remove 5.
Test case 5: all values are <1000, remove 0.
First test case: 999 < 1000, no removal. Second test case: 1000 ≥ 1000, remove the single problem.

solution.approach: For each test case iterate over the N difficulty values and count how many are greater than or equal to 1000. The count is the answer because those are exactly the problems that must be removed. This is O(N) per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) – each difficulty is examined once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
